name: Computer Society
description: Focuses on hardware and software projects from autonomous robots to custom CPUs.
longDescription: The Computer Society (CSociety) is a community of hardware enthusiasts and software engineers dedicated to building complex systems from the ground up. Whether it's designing custom 8-bit CPUs on breadboards or developing high-performance software in Go and C++, CSociety provides a hands-on environment for members to explore the full stack of computing. Our members bridge the gap between abstract computer science theory and practical engineering implementation through rigorous projects and collaborative learning.

sections.title: Build the Future
sections.content: CSociety members work on projects that challenge their understanding of how computers work. From the logic gates of a CPU to the distributed systems of modern web apps, we cover it all. We emphasize a 'learning by doing' approach, where members are encouraged to take ownership of their project components and experiment with new technologies.

items.name: Autonomous Maze-Solving Robot
items.description: A robot that can autonomously navigate and solve complex mazes using sensor fusion and pathfinding algorithms. Built using custom PCB designs and optimized C++ code.

items.name: Custom Wiki (Go)
items.description: A lightweight wiki platform built from scratch in Go, designed for internal knowledge sharing. Features a custom markdown parser and high-concurrency backend.

items.name: 8-bit Breadboard CPU
items.description: A fully functional 8-bit CPU built on breadboards, demonstrating computer architecture fundamentals from simple logic gates up to program execution.

items.question: Do I need prior experience?
items.answer: No! We welcome students of all skill levels. We have projects suitable for beginners and advanced challenges for experienced members. Our senior members provide mentorship to help newcomers get up to speed.

items.question: When do you meet?
items.answer: Meetings vary by project, but we typically have general meetings once every two weeks. Project teams often meet more frequently in the IEEE lab.
